title: How I made 6.4x returns since 2023 (MUST READ)
preview: The following is a complete culmination of my entire findings since I started my investing journey. I have been able to attain a CAGR of 77.36% since 2023 and realize 6.4x returns..

title: Minera Alamos: Why This Should Be an Easy Multibagger by 2027–2028
preview: At roughly US$410M EV, the company is trading at less than one-quarter of the modeled US$1.7B NPV8 modeling of its U.S. assets alone, using a conservative US$4,200 gold assumption.
body:
Minera Alamos is a small gold mining junior that is simply just blatantly undervalued relative to its NAV. They already have production from their Pan Mine, and have several near-term projects that should come online over the next two to three years.

Here is their exact asset portfolio:

1. Pan Mine in Nevada, already producing.
2. Copperstone in Arizona, permitted and expected to become the next producing mine.
3. Gold Rock in Nevada, expected to be developed after Copperstone and connected to the Pan operating complex.
4. Cerro de Oro in Mexico, a major upside project waiting on permits.
5. Additional Mexican assets, valued at zero in the conservative case.

The simple thesis is this: At roughly US$410M EV, the company is trading at less than one-quarter of the modeled US$1.7B NPV8 modeling of its U.S. assets alone, using a conservative US$4,200 gold assumption.

That valuation gives zero credit to the Mexican portfolio side, including the Cerro de Oro mine, meaning investors are effectively paying for the company's Pan, Copperstone, and Gold Rock mining assets at a steep discount while receiving Mexico as a free upside option. In a clear and imminent gold bull market where near-term, low-cost production growth should command a premium in which Minera has, the company looks less like a speculative junior miner play and more like a mispriced emerging intermediate producer hiding in plain sight.

The conservative way to look at the company is to ignore the Mexican assets entirely and ask: what are Pan, Copperstone, and Gold Rock worth by themselves?

Using the NPV models shown in the attached table below, the answer is roughly US$1.7 billion at US$4,200 gold. That compares to a current fully diluted market cap assumption of roughly US$410 million. So even before giving any value to Cerro de Oro or the rest of the Mexico portfolio, the U.S. assets alone suggest the stock could be worth around 4x more than the current market valuation.

That is the entire reason this setup is so attractive. The stock does not need everything to go right to be cheap. It already looks cheap on the U.S. assets alone. Mexico is pure upside.

The U.S. asset valuation: US$1.7 billion NPV8

The attached NPV tables value only the collective U.S. asset base: Copperstone, Pan, and Gold Rock.

The model uses US$4,200 gold. That is important because the valuation is very sensitive to gold prices. At higher gold prices, the value goes up quickly. At lower gold prices, the value goes down. But the key point is that these assets have relatively low operating costs compared to other gold miners, so they still have large margins even if gold pulls back.

The model gives the following values:

Copperstone: US$569.91 million NPV8
Pan Mine: US$433.14 million NPV8
Gold Rock: US$698.53 million NPV8

Total: US$1.70158 billion NPV8

This is the core calculation behind the multibagger case.

If the company's fully diluted market cap is around US$410 million, and the U.S. assets are worth about US$1.7 billion, then the stock is trading at only about 24% of that modeled U.S. asset value. That means the stock could rise roughly 4x just to reach the modeled value of Pan, Copperstone, and Gold Rock.

And again, that gives zero value to Cerro de Oro and the rest of Mexico.

Copperstone: the first major growth catalyst

Copperstone is one of the most important assets because it is expected to be the next mine to come online.

The attached model assumes Copperstone produces 42.5 thousand ounces of gold per year for seven years, plus a small final year of 2.5 thousand ounces. That is around 300 thousand ounces of total modeled production.

At US$4,200 gold, the model estimates that Copperstone can generate US$108.8 million of free cash flow per full production year.

That equals roughly US$2,560 of free cash flow per ounce.

The math is simple:

US$108.8 million of FCF divided by 42.5 thousand ounces equals about US$2,560 per ounce.

The model then discounts those future cash flows at 8% per year. Year 1 is discounted once, which is why US$108.8 million becomes US$100.74 million in present value. Year 2 is discounted more, year 3 more than that, and so on. After adding all those discounted cash flows together, Copperstone gets an NPV8 of US$569.91 million which is a massive number compared to the company's current market value. The same exact NPV pricing calculation is done with the other asset mines

The company's presentation also shows Copperstone as a permitted project with estimated initial capex of only US$36 million, average annual production of roughly 42 thousand ounces, and estimated AISC (AISC means the total cost to produce a single ounce of gold) of US$1,259/oz.

This is why Copperstone matters. It is not a billion-dollar megaproject that requires massive outside financing. It is a relatively low-capex project with high margin potential. If Copperstone enters construction in 2026 and ramps up production in 2027, the market should start valuing Minera Alamos as a multi-mine producer instead of a small one-mine junior.

That is a major rerating event.

Pan Mine: the cash-flow base and downside protection

Pan is already producing, which gives Minera Alamos something many juniors do not have: real cash flow today.

That matters because development-stage miners usually need to raise money by issuing shares and causing dilution. But if Pan is producing cash, Minera can use that cash to directly fund Copperstone, Gold Rock, and eventually Cerro de Oro.

The attached Pan model assumes:

40 thousand ounces of production in Year 1
40 thousand ounces of production in Year 2
20 thousand ounces per year from Years 3 through 10
7 thousand ounces in Year 11

That equals 247 thousand ounces of total modeled production.

The model assumes US$92.8 million of FCF in the 40-thousand-ounce years. That equals about US$2,320 of FCF per ounce.

US$92.8 million divided by 40 thousand ounces equals US$2,320 per ounce. For the 20-thousand-ounce years, the model assumes US$46.4 million of FCF, which is the same per-ounce margin. The first year is not discounted in the Pan table because Pan is already producing. That means the first year's US$92.8 million is treated as current-year cash flow rather than future cash flow.

Adding the discounted cash flows together gives Pan an NPV8 of US$433.14 million.

Pan is important for more than just valuation. It is also the safety net. If gold pulls back, or if Copperstone and Gold Rock take longer than expected, Pan still gives the company operating cash flow. That makes Minera less risky than a pure developer with no producing mine.

Pan also appears to have improved more than the market feared. The concern was that Pan might be near the end of its mine life. But the company updated reserves out to 2029, which suggests Pan can keep contributing cash flow while the next projects are built.

Gold Rock: turning Pan into a larger Nevada production hub

Gold Rock is important because it can potentially feed into the Pan operating complex.

In simple terms, this means Pan is not just one mine. It can become the center of a larger Nevada production system.

The company presentation lists Gold Rock as having average production potential of around 55 thousand ounces per year, with estimated AISC of US$1,008/oz and initial capex of US$64 million.

The attached NPV8 model is more conservative than the company's headline Gold Rock production number. It uses 35 thousand ounces per year for 11 years, plus 18 thousand ounces in the final year. That equals 403 thousand ounces of total production.

At 35 thousand ounces per year, the model assumes Gold Rock generates US$102.74 million of annual FCF.

That equals around US$2,935 of FCF per ounce.

US$102.74 million divided by 35 thousand ounces equals roughly US$2,935 per ounce.

That number is very high because Gold Rock is expected to have a very low cost structure. The company presentation shows estimated AISC of about US$1,008/oz. If gold is US$4,200/oz and costs are near US$1,000/oz, the margin is enormous.

The Gold Rock table discounts the first cash flow twice. That likely reflects the fact that Gold Rock is not expected to produce immediately. It comes later in the project sequence, so the model pushes the cash flows further into the future.

Even with that extra discounting, the model gives Gold Rock an NPV8 of US$698.53 million.

That makes Gold Rock the largest of the three U.S. assets in the model. This is why Gold Rock is such a big part of the thesis. It can extend the life and scale of the Pan complex, increase total production, and add a low-cost Nevada growth asset into the story.

The project timeline is the reason the rerate can happen quickly

This milestone slide is important because it shows that the company has several catalysts packed within a 1-2 year window.

This is not a story where investors need to wait five years for anything to happen. The next 12 to 24 months are full of possible value-unlocking events.

The sequencing slide also shows how the company expects this to unfold operationally.

Pan keeps producing and generating cash flow. Copperstone goes through engineering, construction, and then underground ramp-up into commercial production. Gold Rock moves toward a technical report, construction decision, development, and then production. Cerro de Oro waits for permits, then moves toward an updated technical report, construction, and eventual production.

This matters because the market usually does not fully value future production until it becomes visible and near term. As each project moves from "maybe later" to "being built" to "producing cash," the valuation can rerate upward.

The production growth is the main story

The company's presentation frames Minera Alamos as an emerging intermediate gold producer.

That is the key phrase: A junior producer might produce 30 thousand to 40 thousand ounces per year. An intermediate producer can produce well over 100 thousand ounces per year and usually gets valued at a higher multiple by the market.

The company presentation shows the following production potential:

Pan Operating Complex: 35 thousand to 75 thousand ounces per year
Copperstone: 40 thousand to 45 thousand ounces per year
Cerro de Oro: 55 thousand to 60 thousand ounces per year

Total production potential: 130 thousand to 180 thousand ounces per year, depending on exploration success

So If Minera goes from roughly 30-40 thousand ounces of production to 130-180 thousand ounces, the company is no longer the same junior mining company and the market should not value it the same way.

And because the new mines have lower expected costs, the growth is not just production growth… It is also margin growth.

Why the AISC numbers are so important

AISC is one of the most important numbers in gold mining.

If a company produces gold at US$2,000/oz and gold is US$4,200/oz, it has a US$2,200/oz margin before other corporate-level costs and taxes.

If a company produces gold at US$1,000/oz and gold is US$4,200/oz, it has a US$3,200/oz margin.

That difference is huge.

The company presentation shows:

Pan 2026 AISC guidance: US$1,850–2,000/oz
Copperstone AISC: US$1,259/oz
Gold Rock AISC: US$1,008/oz
Cerro de Oro AISC: US$873/oz

So the growth projects are expected to be much lower cost than Pan. This is very important. It means that as Copperstone, Gold Rock, and Cerro de Oro come online, Minera's average cost per ounce should fall. That means each ounce of production should become more profitable regardless of the current gold spot price per ounce..

At US$4,200 gold, these are extremely high-margin assets…. even higher if gold is $5,000+, which can very very well be justified.. And that is why the stock has so much leverage to gold prices.

Why the low capex matters

A lot of mining projects look great on paper but require hundreds of millions or billions of dollars to build. That creates financing risk. If a junior miner needs a huge amount of money, it may have to issue shares, take on expensive debt, sell royalties, stream future production, or give away part of the project.

Minera's projects are different because the initial capex numbers are relatively low.

The presentation shows:

Copperstone initial capex: US$36 million
Gold Rock initial capex: US$64 million
Cerro de Oro initial capex: US$28 million

These are small numbers relative to the potential value of the assets.

This is one of the biggest reasons Minera is attractive. The company may be able to fund much of this growth through Pan cash flow, Copperstone cash flow, existing cash, debt refinancing, and warrant proceeds.

That lowers the risk of major future dilution.

Why the warrants are not necessarily bad

One of the biggest complaints about Minera Alamos is the large number of warrants at around C$7.00 per share.

A warrant is basically the right to buy shares at a fixed price. If the stock trades above the warrant price, warrant holders may exercise them, meaning they pay the company money and receive shares.

Yes, this creates dilution because more shares exist, but this is not automatically bad. If you are buying the stock below C$7.00, the warrants only become exercisable in-the-money above your purchase price. That means the stock has already gone up before that dilution happens. Also, when the warrants are exercised, cash comes into the company. That cash can help fund Copperstone, Gold Rock, and Cerro de Oro. So, the warrants are better understood as a built-in financing tool. If the stock never gets above the warrant strike, the dilution does not happen. If the stock does go above the warrant strike, shareholders get diluted, but the company receives a large cash injection at a higher share price.

That is much better than the company being forced to raise money at a lower price.This is why I do not view the warrants as bearish if I am buying below the warrant price. I view them as a way to reduce the probability of worse dilution later.

Cerro de Oro is the free call option

The conservative valuation case gives zero value to Cerro de Oro. Cerro de Oro is not needed for the stock to be undervalued. The U.S. assets alone support the thesis. But if Cerro de Oro gets permitted, it could add a lot of value. The company presentation shows Cerro de Oro with:

US$28 million initial capex
Around 58 thousand ounces per year of average production
US$873/oz AISC
US$836 million NPV5 at US$4,500 gold

That is a massive asset compared to the company's current market valuation.

The milestone slide says the Cerro de Oro permit is expected in Q3 2026. If that permit is granted, the market will likely have to start valuing Cerro de Oro as a real development asset instead of a zero, which could cause a major rerating.

This is why I view the Mexican portfolio as a free option. I am not paying for it in the conservative U.S.-asset valuation. But if it works, it can add huge upside.

The recent Orla Mining permitting news in Mexico is also relevant because it suggests the permitting backlog may be starting to clear. If Mexico begins approving more mining permits, Cerro de Oro could move closer to the front of the line.

That does not guarantee approval. But it improves the setup.

The TSX uplisting is another underrated catalyst

The company also plans to graduate from the TSX Venture exchange to the main TSX exchange… this matters more than many people realize.

A lot of funds, institutions, and even some retail investors avoid TSX Venture stocks. Some brokers make it harder or more expensive to buy them. Some investors simply have rules against owning venture-listed companies.

When a company uplists from TSXV to TSX, the investor base can expand. More investors can buy the stock. More funds can look at it. The company can start being compared to a better group of peers. The stock can receive a higher valuation simply because it becomes more investable.

This is especially important if Minera is simultaneously becoming a larger producer.

A TSXV junior with one small producing mine gets one valuation.. but a TSX-listed, multi-mine, American gold producer with 100 thousand-plus ounces of production potential deserves a very different valuation. That is another reason the next couple of years could be powerful.

Why Minera has downside protection

Most junior gold stocks are extremely sensitive to gold price pullbacks.

If gold falls, their project economics can get crushed. Their financing options get worse. Their stocks can fall dramatically because the market realizes they may not be able to build their mines.

Minera is different because it has several layers of downside protection.

First, Pan is already producing.

Second, the growth projects have low initial capex.

Third, Copperstone, Gold Rock, and Cerro de Oro have low expected AISC.

Fourth, the company has a U.S.-heavy asset base, especially after adding Pan, Copperstone, and Gold Rock.

Fifth, the conservative valuation case does not need Mexico to work.

That means even if gold pulls back to US$3,300–3,500/oz, Minera should still be in a much better position than most gold juniors. The margins would be lower, but the projects would likely still make sense because the cost structure is not bloated. This is why I like Minera for the mid-cycle to late-cycle part of the gold bull market. The upside is large if gold keeps rising, but the downside is not as ugly as many other juniors if gold temporarily reverses.

The macro backdrop supports the thesis

The macro reason to own gold has not gone away. The long-term forecast actually looks better than-ever.

The U.S. and other Western governments are running perpetually massive deficits. Money supply expanded dramatically after COVID. Inflation damaged confidence in fiat currencies. The weaponization of the U.S. dollar after Russia's invasion of Ukraine sent a signal to foreign governments that holding U.S. dollars and Treasuries may not be risk-free. Central banks and sovereign buyers have been accumulating gold up until the iran war started, etc

The broad point is simple:

Governments are spending too much, debt levels are too high, and the political incentive is to dilute the debt over time rather than honestly fix the problem.

That is bullish for gold.

If gold averages above US$4,000 over the next three to four years, Minera's assets become extremely valuable. At US$4,200 gold, the attached NPV8 model already supports a U.S. asset valuation of US$1.7 billion. At US$5,000 or US$6,000 gold, the cash flow potential becomes much larger.

This is where the real upside comes from.. A low-cost gold producer does not just benefit from higher gold prices. It benefits disproportionately. For example, if a mine has costs around US$1,200/oz and gold rises from US$4,200 to US$5,200, revenue rises by US$1,000/oz, but the cost does not rise by the same amount. Much of that extra gold price can turn into additional profit and free cash flow.

The jurisdiction premium

Another important point is jurisdiction.

Many gold juniors operate in countries where permitting is slow, governments are unpredictable, taxes can change, communities can block development, and fuel or power shortages can create operational problems.

Minera's growth is increasingly tilted toward the United States, especially Nevada and Arizona. That matters.

If global supply chains become more stressed, or if diesel/fuel availability becomes a bigger issue for miners in weaker jurisdictions, U.S.-based production should become more valuable. Mines in stable North American jurisdictions should trade at a premium because investors will trust the cash flow more.

This is another reason Minera could eventually receive a higher valuation multiple than many other junior miners. The market usually pays more for safer ounces. Gold in Nevada and Arizona should be valued more highly than gold in riskier jurisdictions such as gold in Africa or Brazil..

Why the market may be mispricing the company

The market still seems to be valuing Minera as if Pan might disappoint, Copperstone might not matter yet, Gold Rock is too far away, Cerro de Oro should be ignored, and the warrants are a major problem.

I think that view is wrong.

Pan's reserve update makes the asset more durable than feared. Copperstone is near term. Gold Rock can turn Pan into a larger Nevada production hub. Cerro de Oro is a free upside option. The warrants can help fund development. The TSX uplisting can expand the investor base..and the company's production profile can change dramatically by 2027–2028.

This is why the stock can rerate.

It does not require one single miracle. It requires a series of very visible steps:

Pan keeps producing.
Copperstone gets built.
Gold Rock advances.
Cerro de Oro potentially gets permitted.
The company uplists.
Production grows.
Costs fall.
Free cash flow rises.
The market starts valuing Minera like an emerging intermediate producer instead of a tiny junior.

That is the rerating path.

What can go wrong

This is still mining, so there are real risks.

Copperstone could be delayed. Gold Rock could take longer than expected. Costs could rise. Gold could pull back. Cerro de Oro may not get permitted. Production could underperform. Technical reports could be weaker than expected. The company could still need financing if timelines or costs change.

Bottom line

But the difference with Minera is that the company has multiple different catalyst opportunities and real downside protection.. The company is already producing at Pan. Copperstone is expected to become the next producing mine. Gold Rock can expand the Nevada production base. Cerro de Oro is a large Mexico upside option. The projects have low initial capex, low expected costs, and large margins at current gold prices.

The attached U.S.-asset NPV8 model gives Pan, Copperstone, and Gold Rock a combined value of about US$1.7 billion at US$4,200 gold compared to a fully diluted market cap assumption of roughly US$410 million.

If Cerro de Oro gets permitted, the upside case becomes much larger. If gold stays above US$4,000, the cash flow potential becomes much larger. If the company reaches 130 thousand to 180 thousand ounces of annual production, the market should no longer value it like a small junior.

That is why I think Minera Alamos can be an easy multibagger over the next couple of years.

The market is still looking at it as a small producer with development risk. I think it is actually an emerging North American intermediate gold producer with a funded growth path, multiple near-term catalysts, and a free Mexico call option.

That is the mispricing.

title: Obsidian Energy (OBE): An easy 2-5x play?!
preview: I value Obsidian Energy as an easy 100-400% (2-5x) play from here with a current share price of $11.50 CAD as of 7/2/26
body:
Investment Thesis

The company is Obsidian Energy, a small Canadian E&P. The bet is simple. I value Obsidian Energy as an easy 100-400% (2-5x) play from here with a current share price of $11.50 CAD as of 7/2/26

Per the company's May 2026 website presentation, OBE estimates that their 2P (proved + probable) asset reserves are worth about $2.34B CAD after using a 10% discount rate and base-floor WTI numbers of $75 as the assumptions.. then after subtracting total liabilities and dividing the net assets per-share, OBE suggests the NAV per-share works out to $29.64 and the stock price is currently $11.5 .. so that's already at least a double from just the NAV.

*The reserve numbers are based on the 2025 Reserve Report prepared by GLJ Ltd., an independent qualified reserve evaluator. The February 2026 reserves release says GLJ conducted an independent reserve evaluation of 100% of OBE's reserves, effective December 31, 2025, prepared under Canadian NI 51-101 standards.*

But the NAV/per-share asymmetry is just the start.. Here's the entire setup for this trade:

• Currently trading at 3.43x EV/ 2026 FFO (funds from operations .. aka cash from operations) assuming $75 WTI…. and at 2x EV/ expected 2027 FFO assuming $70 WTI but with higher production coming online

• Oil is looking to be "higher for longer" due to the current oil situation (which I explain towards the end of this).. Even at a WTI floor price of $75 this is still cheap.

• They have a $2.2 billion CAD tax pool that shields the company from paying taxes for roughly a decade at ~ $70 WTI prices. OBE estimates the theoretical value of the pools are about $461m CAD, or $6.85/share CAD, using a 23% tax rate.

• Oil hedges rolling off fast. Their hedge book is falling from 77% in May/June, to 56% in July, to 47% in August, to just 9% by September, allowing them to capture more spot pricing as oil appreciates, meaning more profits.. As of 7/2/26, they have not chosen to hedge prices after September, which I love to see.

• CEO absolutely looooves buybacks.. he has a multi-year track record of maxing out the NCIB for buybacks (roughly 23% of shares retired since 2023 and the CEO joined in 2022).

• The NAV/per-share spread

• CEO ramping up capex spend to increase production and improve cost structure into what could be an 18-24 month or longer oil bull market, with early results already validating the spend (they just spent $100m acquiring a location with a bunch of high-quality light oil barrels and bought it for cheaper than if they were to buy back their own shares..!)

• The capex results for improving their oil wells are looking good! Their drilling and waterflood programs are showing great results. Stronger production, better well results, and improving recovery + decline rates, suggests a multiple expansion is coming

• CEO is very smart and is a great capital allocator.. talks about "per-share" value creation for profitability and somehow always makes great business decisions!

• More capital should start to flow into the energy sector as it makes up only 4% of the total S&P 500 weighting. There should be a rotation into energy whenever capital starts to flow more into risk-off, hard assets.

So essentially here is the play:

The structural tailwind of sustainably higher oil prices of at least $75 WTI + non-hedged pricing kicking in + better business fundamentals from increased capex spend showing promising returns + maxing out share buybacks from the increased cash flows = EPS goes through the roof and you get something similar to what happened with Andean Metals where the underlying commodity appreciated in value and the company's huge buyback operation from its extra cash surplus caused a major momentum rally as EPS exploded..

Cheap based on profitability ??

Ok so it's cheap based on NAV, but what about profitability? Since they are going heavy on capex to increase growth (drilling, infrastructure, waterfloods, expansion) I don't believe they should be valued based on FCF.. This is also actually why the company technically doesn't have a P/E nor a positive FCF number. 2026 Annualized FCF was set to be positive by ~100m CAD, but they raised capex guidance by ~120m so now it's expected to be breakeven - slightly negative because they are spending heavily on reinvestment. They shouldn't be punished for investing in growth for trying to improve their business fundamentals, especially if they realize high returns off the capex.. So I will value them based on EV/FFO and FFO/per-share (FFO means funds from operations, which is essentially cash from operations.. it is the cash the business takes in before accounting for capex)

Based on the company's presentation from their website, their expected 2026 FFO number is around 300m CAD assuming $75 WTI.. So with current market cap of $750m CAD + net debt of 280m = EV of 1.03B .. So an EV/FFO multiple of 3.43..

This is where it gets insane.. 2027 guidance / current valuation is ABSURD

As I'm writing this on 7/2/26, OBE raised their production guidance for this year and next. Here's what stands out to me from their press release today..

Fast growth + insanely cheap valuation…..

They gave a 22% expected increase in production growth from average 2026 to average 2027.

They also gave an average expected average production in 2026 of 30,000 bbls/day.

A 22% increase implies an average 2027 production of 36,600 bbls/day.

OBE is predicting $395 million of debt in 2027, with a debt to FFO ratio of 0.8… This implies 2027 FFO of $493.75 million (call it $500 million).. for reference, their current EV is 1.03b. *calculation for this is FFO = net debt / debt-FFO ratio , so ~ 500m FFO*

That is a lot of cash flow in 2027 and it assumes a low WTI forecast with respect to the oil situation.

What does this all mean??

From prior statements, it is suggested that their maintenance capex spend is estimated at about $240m for 40,000 bbls/day.

To keep this simple (and conservative), I'll assume maintenance capital is about $240m for 36,600 bbls/day. This is consistent with OBE's expected $120m capex for H1 2027 (approximately maintenance capital).

So if we take the implied FFO of $493.75 and subtract the maintenance capital spend of $240m, we get $253.75m remaining.

Now imagine if OBE initiated a 50% payout ratio dividend..

Meaning, if OBE paid half of that $253.75 million out as a dividend, and retained the other half for debt reduction/growth/other corporate purposes, then OBE would have about $126.9 for a dividend.

OBE presently has about 66 million shares outstanding. They have about 5 and a bit shares in prepaid equity forwards. If these forwards were exercised, and the shares cancelled (part of an NCIB), the net number of shares would be about 61 million.

$126 million paid as a dividend to about 61 million shares, gives a dividend just over $2 per share.

Ie, the information provided in the July 2/26 news release, provides a growth rate, debt and implied trailing FFO, that is forecast to take them to a production rate at which a 50% dividend payout ratio would result in a $2 dividend.

A $2 dividend would significantly revalue the share price. For example the current share price is in the mid $11 range.

A $2 dividend, with a 7% dividend yield, would mean a mid $28 share price. We can see this from simple dividend math, where $2/0.07 = $28.57 share price.

I'm not claiming this is going to happen. I am saying OBE is forecasting itself to be in a position one year from now, where they would be able to pay a $2 dividend (using a 50% payout ratio).

Given the share price impact a $2 manageable dividend (and continued production growth/debt reduction) would have, I suspect if the share price is meaningly below $28 at the time, OBE mgmt would be under a lot of pressure from shareholders to implement such a dividend.

Regardless, a $500 million FFO with 61 million net shares, is about $8.2/share FFO… Current share price is $11.44 as I'm writing this.. That is about 1.4X 2027 forecasted FFO, which is a super low FFO multiple.

A 3x FFO ratio (also low), and would need a share price of about $24.6.. 4X FFO would need a $32.8 share price…

Talk about a dirt-cheap company that's growing production and is about to face a massive oil re-pricing tailwind.

Hedging

Given today's WTI price, OBE is well hedged in July and August. I suspect that may be enough to get OBE through the current low oil price period regardless of what happens in the Middle East.

By regardless, I mean even if Iran and the US become best friends, and live happily ever after, oil may stabilize at a higher number. I'm not in that camp. In my view, the Iran/US conflict is far from over. It has a number of rounds yet to go before the loser understands they can't win, and give up to the winner.. so either Iran submits to US demands, or the US goes away. Neither of which has yet happened.

Given my view, I'm very happy OBE has not decided to hedge further so far (as of 7/2/26) at current low prices.

$75 WTI as a floor, but what if crude goes higher ???

Rising WTI prices disproportionately flow to the bottom line. Almost every expense in OBE's cost structure (operating costs, transportation, G&A, interest, sustaining capital) is fixed in dollar terms and doesn't move much when crude oil prices rise.. Only royalties go up modestly. This means that incremental oil revenue falls through to cash flow almost untouched by costs.. and keep in mind that OBE doesn't have to pay taxes for 10 years..

Management's own guidance says every US$1/bbl move in WTI changes 2026 FFO by roughly $3.9m CAD, and that sensitivity should get even larger once the hedge book rolls off and OBE starts to capture more full spot pricing rather than a blended, partially-hedged number.

But essentially a hypothetical move from $80 WTI to $120 WTI would mean $40 × 3.9m or roughly $155m CAD of incremental 2026 FFO annualized.. or about $77m if adjusted only for 2026 Q3 and Q4.. if we take the semiannual number and add it to the expected 300m 2026 FFO, the new EV/FFO multiple is now ~ 2.78… Again this is just hypothetical, the point of this is to illustrate how powerful a potential oil spike can be in terms of affecting valuation metrics.. in this case, the EV/FFO multiple would fall from 3.43 to 2.78, a 65 basis point differential for the multiple..

Why the buyback story matters so much

With the extra cash that OBE would get from the oil price tailwind, management would absolutely be doing buybacks, and especially if the stock remains dead cheap. The renewed annual NCIB allows repurchase of up to 6.46M shares, equal to 10% of the public float, and the company had already maxed its prior NCIB, buying 7.14M shares at about $7.16/share CAD. They even talked about doing a substantial issuer bid last year (which is what you have to do after maxing a normal course issuer bid). They ended up shelving that idea, but that's the point, it's on the shelf!

One angle also worth flagging that often gets missed is that in a sustained $100+ oil environment where the buyback story becomes obvious to the market, Canadian E&P names don't trade at 3-5x FFO (current EV/FFO for OBE is 3.43x) .. they trade HIGHER (probably at least 6-7x). Look at what Andean Metals did once the FCF/share narrative landed.. the multiple ripped, not just the cash flow.

So if OBE compounds buybacks for 12-18 months AND the tax pool benefit becomes obvious to analysts, you could see a 6x+ multiple, not 3.5x.. And at a 6x multiple with annual 2027 FFO of 500m CAD (based on expected 2027 guidance) and with 55M shares projected, that's $54.55/share compared to $11.5/share today.. (500 x 6 / 55m = $54.55 CAD) These calculations also don't even fully realize the firm's NAV, so the upside is even higher.

Waterflood upside.. the hidden catalyst

There's also a ton more upside that doesn't even show up in the EV/FFO model… im talking about their waterflood pilots.

To explain what a waterflood is for the oil industry, imagine a sponge that's soaked in oil. If you just squeeze it, you can get some of the oil out, but the sponge is full of tiny pores and a lot of oil stays trapped inside, no matter how hard you squeeze. You can maybe only get 10% of the oil out before the sponge stops releasing any more. Now imagine pouring water into one side of the sponge while continuing to squeeze the other side. The water starts to push through the sponge, displacing the trapped oil and pushing it out the other side. Suddenly you can recover way more oil than just squeezing alone.

This is exactly what a waterflood does. A normal oil well gets maybe 5-10% of the oil out of the ground before the reservoir's natural pressure runs out and production starts to decline fast, but a waterflood pumps water into nearby injection wells to push the trapped oil back toward the producing wells.

If OBE proves this works at scale, the reserves they can recover from the same wells essentially doubles or triples without even drilling a single new well. This means the company's reserve base would also be revised higher (maybe 50-100%). Decline rates would also fall, well lives get extended by years, and the market starts treating OBE like a long-life conventional producer which trades at 5-7x FFO instead of a high-decline shale-style E&P which trades at 3-4x FFO.

So far, waterflood is working !!

The waterflood evidence starts with the Dawson 04-24 pilot. In April 2026, Obsidian said two additional injection wells began operating in the field in early February, bringing the Dawson 04-24 pilot to four injectors and three producers. The company said this pilot had been fully operational for about two months.

Two things were tracked to see if it was working: whether the injector wells could keep pumping water in at a steady rate and whether the producer wells' decline slowed down.. Fortunately, the injectors DID manage to maintain a consistent injection rate, and decline rates DID flatten instead of dropping like a normal well. This does not yet prove ultimate recovery uplift, but it strongly suggests reservoir communication and pressure support are working.

Then in Q1 2026, because the early signs looked good, management didn't just keep testing.. they started to pour in capex. Substantial amounts of capex. They expanded injection further at Dawson, started building two more pilot projects elsewhere (Nampa, West Dawson), and then in June committed an extra $100m which was added to the 2026 budget, with $32m specifically to build out Dawson further with 10 more injectors, 6 more producers, plus supporting infrastructure… Given that management went from testing to committing serious capex, the data is legitimately encouraging.

If Dawson waterflood works, it completely changes the company's heavy crude asset portfolio quality. Primary heavy-oil wells can have steep declines, but a successful waterflood can flatten declines, increase recovery, and make the production base more durable.. So if the waterflood works, less capital would be needed to replace declines and more capital can go towards real, increased growth.

Why this is the multibagger optionality

Asset reserve revaluation (NAV increases): Every successful waterflood pilot adds extra bookable proved + probable reserves. The 2025 reserve report that was mentioned in the very beginning of the write-up already included the initial waterflood reserves from the Dawson 4-24 pilot. If Nampa and West Dawson also work, the 2026 reserve report (out February 2027) will book substantially more..
Higher reserves = higher fair value.

Decline rate reduction: OBE's own 2026 guidance specifically notes that "the waterflood spend is driving a reduction to the projected 2027 decline rate." Lower decline rates mean lower sustaining capex requirements. Today OBE spends ~ $175m/yr worth of capex just to hold production flat. If decline rates fall meaningfully, that sustaining number drops, and every dollar saved is a dollar that flows straight through for more cash to spend.

Multiple expansion: The market currently prices OBE as an E&P with steep well decline rates. Successful waterflood systems transform the company into a longer-life producer with much lower decline rates. That alone justifies a moderate multiple re-rating (3.5x - 5-6x FFO) that isn't baked into any analyst forecasts.

It should be noted that the waterflood thesis is largely independent of the oil price thesis. If oil collapses back to $70 WTI, waterflood still works.. it just means the resulting reserves are worth less per barrel. Ideally the two catalysts get stacked together, where you get both the oil price upside AND the waterflood upside..

The results from the rest of OBE's "well-maintenance" capex spend have also been really promising. Here's the data as of the end of June, 2026 (per their official website)

1. HVS waterflood regeneration is starting to look exciting!
2. West Dawson new wells are solid
3. New Open Creek Belly River wells still cleaning up
4. Crimson Belly River looks better than Open Creek.

HVS (Harmon Valley South) is part of Obsidian's Peace River heavy oil asset. The wells they've drilled there keep coming in strong, which suggests this field still has real life left in it (not running out of good drilling locations).. In H1 2025, all five planned wells at HVS came online. Two of them used a new "waffle well" design which worked and boosted initial production on pads that were already drilled. Early production rates (IP30 = average rate over the first 30 days) were solid across the board:

HVS 13-08: 440 and 350 boe/d
HVS 13-18: 251 and 185 boe/d
HVS 14-07: 568 boe/d (IP22, so first 22 days)

For heavy oil wells, these are strong numbers. In Q3 2025, They drilled two more wells at the HVS 14-07 pad, targeting a different rock layer. These averaged 385 boe/d in their first 30 days, and importantly, 100% oil (no water or gas diluting the number).

So, HVS is a good proof point that Obsidian's Peace River drilling inventory isn't tapped out.. new wells and new techniques (like the waffle design) are still producing strong results. This suggests that OBE can keep growing production profitably on top of whatever additional results the waterflood program adds separately.

West Dawson: In April 2026, Obsidian said the West Dawson 09-21 Clearwater pad was producing significantly above expectations in terms of API and viscosity, which matters because with heavy oil, better API and lower viscosity usually means easier flow, better economics, and potentially better waterflood behavior.

But West Dawson isn't just a couple of lucky wells.. as I just mentioned it's becoming its own waterflood project, being built by the lessons from Dawson.

In April 2026, Obsidian had already drilled the injector wells next to West Dawson's producer wells and was getting them ready to start "commissioning" which just means testing and prepping equipment before it goes live. They also said West Dawson (and Nampa) would use an improved layout with better spacing and facility design based on what they learned from running the original Dawson pilot. And just this month in June, the company said injection at West Dawson was about to start any day now.

This is super encouraging because Obsidian is treating this location as the next deliberate waterflood build using a smarter, more advanced design system because they already learned what works and what doesn't at Dawson. Ultimately this would place the West Dawson plots with the same upside potential that Dawson is already displaying, which is showing better recovery rates and flatter declines.

"The new open creek belly river wells still cleaning up".. This is a different part of the company than the Peace River waterflood stuff. Open Creek is in Willesden Green which is OBE's light oil area (not the heavy oil fields in Peace River). Here they're targeting a formation called Belly River, and trying to prove it can be a second, repeatable growth zone alongside the Cardium zone they already produce from.

When a new well first comes online, it's still flushing out the fluids used to drill and complete it. Rates may look low or unstable at first and then rise as the well settles into its natural flow. That's normal and expected, but it also means early numbers can understate what a well will actually produce once stabilized.

So here's what actually happened:

A two-well pad (Open Creek 11-28) started producing in early March 2026, and rates kept climbing week over week, exactly what you'd expect during cleanup.

Its first-30-day average was 303 boe/d per well. But in the most recent 10-day stretch, it was doing 463 boe/d per well, meaningfully higher than the initial number.

Another well (Open Creek 06-33) showed the same pattern: 199 boe/d average in its first 30 days, rising to 288 boe/d by its first 120 days.

With a brand-new play like this, the risk is usually that wells look great for a moment and then fade. Here it's the opposite.. wells are getting BETTER after their initial numbers, not worse. That suggests the standard "IP30" (first 30-day average) headline numbers are actually underselling how good these wells really are.

The bigger picture here is that if Belly River holds up as a repeatable, viable zone, it adds a whole new bucket of drilling locations to Willesden Green on top of what they already have there (105 net reserve locations, ~12,000 boe/d of current production, and a manageable ~15% annual decline rate)

"Crimson Belly river looks better than Open Creek".. the numbers support this, but only barely, and with one important caveat.. it's based on just one well..

Crimson's first-ever Belly River horizontal well (drilled Q3 2025) came in at 538 boe/d in its first 25 days, 76% oil, a good number.

Open Creek's Belly River wells, by comparison, started lower: 303 boe/d and 199 boe/d in their first 30 days.

So yes, on headline early rates, Crimson's well beat anything Open Creek has shown so far. Crimson is already a core, well-understood part of OBE's operations. This well was essentially them testing whether a second productive zone (Belly River) exists underneath ground they already know well.

However, OBE announced they are drilling 14 net Belly River wells across both Crimson and Open Creek in the second half of 2026, so clearly both areas are performing well enough to justify accelerating. The catch is that one well is just one well. Open Creek has multiple wells with a track record of improving over time which is reassuring, but Crimson only has a single strong data point so far.. I guess we will see what happens here. I have full faith in the CEO so I view this as good news.

Ok but if there's so much good news and developments, then why has revenue declined ~30% over the past year ????

This is what troubled me when I first saw their income statement history, but it turns out that there's a good explanation for this.

99% of the revenue decline is from one transaction: the Pembina disposition in April 2025. OBE sold its operated Pembina assets to InPlay Oil Corp in April 2025 for $325m total consideration ($211M cash + 9.1M InPlay shares + $15M working interest). Those assets were producing ~11,000 boe/d at the time of sale..roughly 30% of OBE's total production.

When you remove 30% of production, revenue obviously drops, but the sale was actually a smart choice. OBE's current asset portfolio is actually performing better than how the Pembina assets were, in terms of the economics on a per-barrel basis… Pembina were mature, declining assets with high decommissioning obligations and average per-barrel economics. The cash from the sale was deployed into Peace River Clearwater (the highest-return inventory currently in OBE's portfolio) and Willesden Green Belly River just now (the light oil growth platform). Also from the sale, net debt dropped from $411.7m to $268m in 8 months and cash went towards buybacks. Without the Pembina sale, OBE would likely still be carrying $400m+ in debt during the current oil shock, which would've limited their flexibility to do buybacks, acquisitions, and fund their waterflood capex..

But more importantly this also tells a lot about the CEO and that he really means what he says. Remember in the intro when I mentioned he emphasizes "per-share value creation?".. That's exactly what happened here. The revenue line went down by 30%…. A huge, huge amount that would frighten the living sh*t out of most CEO's, but not this guy.

Quality over quantity. It was wise to sell a mature, declining asset at a good price to fund growth in better assets for the future, which they have done.

The Main Risks

The biggest risk here ironically is if the price of oil goes up too much. That is, if the oil shock is so bad that the result is a global depression that completely crushes global demand for oil. I believe this is definitely a possibility, but there's no way of assessing the odds of this happening.. In the event that this does happen, oil may fall to $50 for quite some time or remain high at $200.. there's no way to really know. But, the factor that mitigates this risk is that most of the current SPR releases are based on contract agreements that are set to be paid back between November 2026 and September 2028 and with a 20% surplus paid as extra barrels of oil (so if 10 barrels got released, the borrower has to return 12 total).. And if we look at the data, the total global SPR releases that happened since April were 220-270 mmbbls not including China, which is plenty of supply that has to be produced and paid back. Also, countries will want to refill their commercial + SPR reserve inventories, and this time with even more supply, which just means that there is a lot of pent-up demand for oil (more on this soon)

Another risk is differentials, not just headline WTI. OBE has meaningful heavy oil exposure, and WCS differentials can historically widen in ugly ways. Their updated 2026 assumptions use US$15 WCS differential, and their sensitivity table says a US$1/bbl WCS differential move changes FFO by about $2.3m CAD… HOWEVER, due to the Straight of Hormuz's extended closure and with traffic not even close to being normalized (tension all over the place as of 7/1/26), heavy/sour crude is now supply constrained globally.. This resulted in the WCS differential actually tightening somewhat, so this risk is a little overblown here.

Third risk: management may choose growth capex over buybacks. They just raised 2026 capex by $100m–$125m CAD, so near-term FCF may look weaker if they are intentionally using the cash for drilling and waterflooding.

Fourth risk: the oil macro call can be right directionally but a bit overexaggerated.. If WTI stays around $75–$85, the stock is still cheap I think, but the explosive buyback + huge FCF = EPS squeeze version probably needs sustained prices materially at or above $100.

Now begins what people call the hard part.. Macro analysis of oil

Ok there's a lot of fake news out online and nothing really makes sense right now as of 7/1/2026, but here are the facts:

• Global commercial inventories + SPR reserve levels are at all time low's
• Russia is considering a diesel export ban, several of their refineries were hit by Ukraine.. many Middle Eastern wells have also been shut in with some refineries damaged
• Chinese imports have collapsed by 5-6m barrels/day, massively suppressing the official demand for oil numbers..
• Iran is currently trying to dump its pre-existing oil to make a nice profit before they start producing more oil, but they can't even get rid of all of it
• Tankers that were stuck inside the strait are leaving, but very few are going in to refill.. Tankers say "it's too risky" and freight costs have skyrocketed up over 4x normal costs.. meaning even major refiners are having trouble securing bids for available tankers.. Speaking of this, there are also plants citing cutting production or suspending pumping entirely because of the lack of available tankers
• Cushing is officially at tank bottoms in USA, they are trying to redirect inventory from other hubs into Cushing to prop it up
• USA has been supplying Europe + Japan with oil from exports funded with SPR, but SPR will not last forever. USA imports a major amount of their heavy crude usage (the oil that jet fuel, distillate, diesel is made out of) from Canada, but Canada is allegedly running low on inventory too
• The strait traffic is low because there are "designated pathways" that limits ship passage due to mines that have yet to start being removed, which takes months to get cleared.
• 9-10m barrels/day of shut-in production across all gulf countries
• 50% of oil leaving Strait is from trapped tankers.. the other 50% is from new oil loading
• Energy demand is not flinching at all given oil prices are below $70… means shortages will come even faster and be worse.
• Crack spreads are at an ALL TIME HIGH vs crude, meaning that the things made from crude (gasoline, diesel, jet fuel, distillates) are exploding up in price with margins per barrel of over $55 vs current oil price per barrel of < $70 !!! So either these huge crack spreads are fraudulent are temporary or the price of crude is just ridiculously low…
• There is massive pent up demand globally for oil and oil products

This is the chart that amazes me the most lately: 3-2-1 crude oil crack spreads not only continue to break new all-time highs, but at ~$58, the spread is just TEN DOLLARS AWAY IN ABSOLUTE AMOUNT from 1 barrel of WTI Crude at ~68$

The irony behind this:

Question 1: If oil refiners are supposedly printing money as if there is no tomorrow while having access to ample supply of cheap crude oil, why are the shares of oil refiners ( $CRAK ) only up 6% since Feb. 27th - July 2nd ?

Question 2: How can you have a potential shortage of gasoline and diesel when refineries have been broadly running almost at 100% capacity around the world to replace the lost output from the Middle East, without demand growing in the past 4 months?

Question 3: Why aren't inventories being refilled now that the price of crude oil is back at pre-war levels and "everything is back to normal"?

But to really emphasize just how ridiculous these crack spreads are and how the implied value of oil should be much, much higher as a result.. pay no further attention to these two images below which seem to add up and suggest that today's current "fair value" of oil (as of 7/2/26) should be around $115 - $125.. keep in mind that as of 7/2/26, the crack spreads are at ALL TIME HIGHS of $61 (and not what is displayed in the images because they are slightly outdated.. they show spreads of mid-high $50's so keep this in mind..) and are projected to keep rising.

And, to really put how absurd all this is into context with respect to history, look at this table below…

No one knows what's going on right now. No one knows why Chinese refiners aren't buying crude from the market now at these ridiculously cheap prices (might be from ridiculously expensive freight costs, but who knows), and no one knows why oil futures prices appear to be handicapped, but one thing is certain:

We still have a 9m daily deficit of shut-in production that is being funded with unsustainable SPR drawdowns. When that oil ends, and it will end eventually if nothing materially changes.. where will the oil come from? Hormuz is handicapped.. Also most of the Iranian oil from the Gulf has been going to China to recharge their inventories (meaning it's also not going to other countries like Japan, Korea, India, etc that desperately need oil too) which is what we should be doing.. But we're not. We're draining them down like there's no tomorrow, with no real plan in sight. So as no real developments likely continue to happen within the Strait of Hormuz, inventories will continue to get depleted, which means more pent-up demand.

There's also an emerging phenomenon I want to highlight, which is the emergence of new policies to ensure fuel shortages can't occur in the future.

What do I mean? Well, let's just look at Australia. The opposition called on the federal government to double minimum fuel reserves held in Australia and boost storage capacity in response to the global oil crisis. This fuel policy would mean Australia always has at least 60 days of diesel, petrol and jet fuel on shore, but the plan would add to the cost of fuel.

The thing is, we're in the first stages of this fuel crisis. Here's what Australia already plans: a permanent onshore reserve of 1 billion litres. That's a new SPR that didn't exist before. That's new demand, that's yet to be filled, or even ordered.

To summarize, Labor has committed A$10 billion to lift onshore fuel security, including a new government-owned reserve of 1 billion litres. The policies also raise the private minimum stockholding obligation by about 10 days. The proposal goes further: it would require Australia to reach 60 days of diesel, petrol and jet fuel onshore by 2030, supported by an $800 million Australian Fuel Security Facility and at least 1 billion litres of new storage..

This is just Australia. A lot more countries that can afford this will likely also be doing the same.

And the thing really is though, even if a ceasefire somehow does actually manage to be achieved and sustained, do we really believe that oil stays $70 WTI??? The same as pre-war levels?? I really don't think so.. the greatest energy crisis of our lifetimes is not over! Yes, there is a temporary "glut" from unsanctioned Iranian barrels + unlocked Strait vessels, but that is temporary. With inbound traffic 1/4 of normal, 8.6m barrels/day of still curtailed production, US SPR near operational minimums, and an inevitable return of Chinese buying, I believe the downside from here is extremely minimal. Likewise, OBE's CEO seems like a seriously smart capital allocator, and the upside is a super easy multibagger once the real oil prices start to show up.

title: Saylor's Ponzi EXPLAINED.. WHY BTC IS ABOUT TO COLLAPSE
preview: Saylor's firm MSTR is essentially a levered bitcoin fund that borrows money from investors.. The fund itself does not produce any income but rather relies on Bitcoin to keep appreciating.

title: WHY WE WILL HAVE ANOTHER 70'S COMMODITY BULL RUN AND WHY OIL WILL BE HIGHER FOR LONGER
preview: When you look at the 70's decade commodity bull run, question WHY it happened.. Commodities back then were hit by real supply shocks and m2 was going rampant.. sound familiar?
